coredata.dc:title: Effect of a compressed separator on the electrochemical performance of Li-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wei
Li, Q.M.
Xiao, Ping
Carbone, Paola
coredata.dc:description: This work investigates the effects of compressed separators on the electrochemical performance of Li-ion batteries (LIBs) and the underpinning mechanisms. Two typical commercial separators (polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE)) with different compressive behaviours were used in this study. The tortuosity values of compressed PP and PE separators were determined using Electrochemical Impedance Spectrum (EIS) tests, showing that the PP separator has a higher tortuosity than that of the PE separator under given compressive stress. LIBs with the compressed PP and PE separators were tested for their capacity performance. It is found that the onset strain of densifications can be considered as the critical compressive strain for the significant reduction of LIB's capacity. The experimental observations are supported by COMSOL modelling results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
Separator
Compression
Densification
Tortuosity
Porosity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Strain at the onset of densification of separators
Variation of porosity and tortuosity under uniaxial compression
Electrochemical performance of half-cell assembled with compressed separator

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic drawing of the coin cell for EIS testing (spacer|separator|spacer) for determining the tortuosity of compressed separator, (b) The schematic drawing of the half-cell for electrochemical performance testing associated with the compressed separator.

The typical representative stress-strain curve of separators under interrupted compression testing with loading and unloading at a strain-rate of 10−3 s−1: (a) PP Celgard 2500; (b) PE Hipore 25; (c) Permanent strain at 24 h after unloading for PP and PE separators under specified loading stress; (d) 3D microstructure of uncompressed PP Celgard 2500 reconstructed from XCT image technique (light colour is polymer phase and dark colour is pore phase); (e) Surface view of PP separator before compression; (f) Surface view of PP separator after 90 MPa compression; (g) 3D microstructure of uncompressed PE Hipore 25 reconstructed from XCT image technique (light colour is polymer phase and dark colour is pore phase); (h) Surface view of PE separator before compression; (i) Surface view of PE separator after 90 MPa compression. (For the colour references in this figure legend, the reader should refer to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Stress-strain curves of PP and PE separators defined with three regimes: elastic, plateau and densification; (b) energy efficiency-strain curves of PP and PE separators to determine the onset strain of densification.

(a) Porosity-strain curves of PP and PE separators obtained from Eq. (8); (b), (c) Nyquist plots of configured coin cells with compressed PP and PE separators filled with electrolyte, respectively, to determine the resistances of cells (R
cell ) at specified stresses; (d) Tortuosity-strain curve of the compressed PP and PE separators; (e) the fitted tortuosity factor-porosity curves for the PP and PE separators by eliminating strain in (a) and (d) to determine the scaling factor “
γ
” and Bruggeman expoenent “α”.

The representative charge (dashed line) and discharge (solid line) curves under different C-rates of half-cell assembled with compressed PP separators at specified compressive stresses: (a) 0 MPa; (b) 20 MPa; (c) 30 MPa; (d) 40 MPa; (e) 60 MPa; (f) 90 MPa.

The representative charge (dashed line) and discharge (solid line) curves under different C-rates of half-cell assembled with compressed PE separators at specific stresses: (a) 0 MPa; (b) 20 MPa; (c) 30 MPa; (d) 40 MPa; (e) 60 MPa; (f) 90 MPa.
Capacity-strain curve of half-cell with compressed separators at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 4C: (a). Compressed PP separator; (b) Compressed PE separator.

(a) Schematic drawing of the 2D axisymmetric geometry used in the simulation; (b)–(c) The voltage losses across the compressed PP (b) and PE (c) separators, respectively, at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C and 4C; (d) variation of the normalized voltage loss with the non-dimensional number (
τ 2
L ‾
1
Φ
) based on Fig. 8.
The compressive strains of PP and PE separator samples after unloading from specified compressive loading stresses.
Parameters of cathode and electrolyte used for COMSOL simulation.

row.entry: Initial LiPF6 concentration in the electrolyte [M]

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Input parameters of partially compressed separators for COMSOL simulation.

head.ce:title: Effect of a compressed separator on the electrochemical performance of Li-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Manchester, M13 9PL, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Manchester M13 9PL, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials, Henry Royce Institute, The University of Manchester, Manchester, M13 9PL, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Manchester, M13 9PL, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Engineering, The University of Manchester, Manchester M13 9PL, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work investigates the effects of compressed separators on the electrochemical performance of Li-ion batteries (LIBs) and the underpinning mechanisms. Two typical commercial separators (polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE)) with different compressive behaviours were used in this study. The tortuosity values of compressed PP and PE separators were determined using Electrochemical Impedance Spectrum (EIS) tests, showing that the PP separator has a higher tortuosity than that of the PE separator under given compressive stress. LIBs with the compressed PP and PE separators were tested for their capacity performance. It is found that the onset strain of densifications can be considered as the critical compressive strain for the significant reduction of LIB's capacity. The experimental observations are supported by COMSOL modelling results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high energy densities and efficiency, Li-ion batteries (LIBs) have proven their superiority over other energy storage technologies, and therefore, have been widely used in electronic devices and electrical vehicles (EVs). A LIB comprises anode, cathode, separator and electrolytes [1]. Although the separator in LIB does not participate in the electrochemical reaction, it plays vital roles in preventing contact between anode and cathode and facilitating ion transport. The mechanical deformation of separator could potentially lead to a short circuit [2,3] and impede ion transport, which can further influence LIB's safety and performance [4–6]. Thus, the mechanical properties of the separator have attracted great attentions from researchers.

During the daily use of a LIB, the separator should have appropriate mechanical properties to withstand various external and internal loads. For example, the separator experiences external loading when the EV has a crush accident or when the mobile device drops to the ground. The internal stack pressure is applied to the separator during the assembly to ensure intimate contact among LIB's components, which could reach up to 5 MPa [7]. In the cycle of charging and discharging, separators are subjected to the internal compressive loading (i.e. active stress) from the volumetric expansion of electrode materials inside the LIB, which is a dynamic process, and the associated active stress may vary from 1.5 to 3 MPa [7]. For instance, the cathode material of lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO2) could have 1.9% volume expansion during charging [8] while the anode material of graphite can expand 10% in volume [9]. The use of novel electrode materials may produce even higher volume changes during the charging-discharging cycle, e.g. a high-capacity anode material made of silicon can expand as high as 400% of its volume [10]. Although the usual active stress is relatively low to close the pore of the separator, the reduced pore space of the separator would certainly reduce the ionic conductivity of the separator immersed in the electrolyte, especially when high capacity and charging rate are pursued. In the later stage of the LIB life span, the compressive deformation of the separator may become excessive, i.e. Lagadec et al. [11] have experimentally shown that the separator can be locally compressed from 16 μm to 10 μm (37.5% engineering compressive strain) after a LIB experiences 100 charging-discharging cycles in this particular case. Furthermore, any mechanical abuse of the LIB (e.g. by a local impact or in a vehicle crash) could introduce even larger compressive deformation to the separator [12,13]. Therefore, it is necessary to understand the influence of the compressive deformation of the separator on the mechanical behaviour of the separator and the performance of LIB.

Various experiments have been conducted to study the mechanical behaviour of the separator under compressive loading conditions [14–19]. Cannarella et al. [14] investigated the compressive and tensile mechanical properties of separator (Celgard 3501) under various strain-rates and different fluid environments. They highlighted the anisotropic nature of separators and the importance of measuring the compressive properties in addition to the tensile properties. Subsequently, they developed a model to predict the elastic response of the separator immersed in the fluid subjected to compression at different strain-rates [15]. Their model shows that the elastic response of the separator at different strain-rates is determined by the combination of viscoelasticity of the polymer skeleton and the poroelastic effect of fluid flow in the pores. Zhang et al. [16] studied the mechanical behaviours including material strength and failure of four types of separators. In their uniaxial compressive testing, it was found that the dry-processed polyethylene (PE) and triple-layer separators show significant anisotropic properties, while the wet-processed ceramic-coated and nonwoven separators show much lower anisotropy. Kalnaus et al. [17] studied the temperature and strain-rate sensitivities of the triple-layer separator (Celgard 2325) using the digital image correlation (DIC) technique. The testing results show the strain-rate hardening of the compressive stress of the separator and its decrease with the increase of temperature. However, most of these previous studies were performed at macroscopic scale without linking the macroscopic compressive behaviour of a separator to its microstructure and considering the influence of the compressed separator on the performance of LIB.

Until recently, researchers start to build the connections between macroscopic compressive behaviour and microscopic characteristics of separators. For example, Yu et al. [18] investigated the influences of the compression (at 35 MPa) on the microporous morphologies, electrolyte uptake behaviour and electrochemical properties of three types of commercial separators using Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) and X-ray diffraction measurements. The results indicate that the microporous structures of separators are important for the structural stability of the separator, and therefore, the compressive deformation of a separator influences its electrochemical performance. Furthermore, Ding et al. [19] analysed the mechanical behaviour, microstructure evolution and the variation of mechanical properties of three kinds of commercial separators under compressive loading. The experimental results show that the separator made from a dry-processed uniaxial stretching has better resistance to compressive loading than the other two separators made from dry-/wet-processed biaxial stretching due to their different microstructures. Later, they took the effects of temperature on the compressive behaviours of dry-processed PP into consideration and found that the PP separator is softened and has worse compressive properties with increased temperature [20]. They also proposed a method, where the commercial PE separator is modified by impregnated with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), to improve electrochemical and compressive behaviour (up to 25 MPa) [21]. Recently, Ding et al. [22] investigated the compressive behaviour and ion transport variation (up to 40 MPa) of PP and PE separators with considering strain-rate, fluid-immersed and cycling effects. The experimental results showed that the compressed PP separator exhibits excellent compression resistance and higher ionic conductivity retention in comparison with compressed PE separator. It is evident that these existing studies only considered the effects of relatively small compression of the separator on electrochemical performance (35 MPa in Ref. [18], 25 MPa in Ref. [21], 40 MPa in Ref. [22]), which does not cover the likely scenarios of the pore closure of the separator under moderate to large compressive deformations (up to 90 MPa). Furthermore, although the porosity variations of separators were experimentally characterized for different compressive stresses [18–22], the tortuosity, as a key parameter to determine the mass and Li-ion transfers in the separator of LIB, which has been widely used for homogenised modelling of battery performance in commercial software (e.g. COMSOL Multiphysics [23]), was not evaluated.

On the other hand, separators subjected to moderate to high local compressive deformation have been studied with the consideration of the local pore closure and its effect on lithium plating using experimental and numerical methods [24–26]. Recently, Yuan et al. [27] proposed an in-situ method to detect the pore closure defect of the separator, which is based on the observation of abnormal voltage variation when the battery with defected separator is rested in 24 h after fully charged. In their study, the equivalent effect of separator's pore closure defect is produced by sticking a PE terephthalate tape on the anode, which, however, cannot represent the effects of the compressively deformed separator. In addition, the experimental setup for the defected separator only considered the two extreme cases, i.e., no defect case and an equivalent complete pore closure case. However, the evolution of the pore space of the separator during compression is a continuous process between these two extreme cases. Therefore, it is necessary to understand the effect of the partially compressed separator on the electrochemical performance of LIB.

The effects of compressed separator on the battery performance in a wide range of compressive stress (or deformation) are studied in the present paper using two partially compressed typical separators, i.e., the polypropylene (PP) separator made from the dry-processed uniaxial stretching method and the PE separator made from the wet-processed biaxial stretching method. Firstly, the interrupted mechanical compressive testing was conducted for both separators to analyse their deformation evolution and obtain the partially compressed separator samples. Then, the compressed separators were characterized using porosity and tortuosity via the analytical prediction and impedance measurement, respectively. Subsequently, the C-rate performance of LIB assembled with the compressed separator was tested and analysed, which was evaluated qualitatively using a 2D axisymmetric model of LIB built in COMSOL Multiphysics software. Finally, a critical compression strain for the start of decrease of LIB's capacity performance is identified and determined.

Commercial separators of monolayer Celgard 2500 and monolayer Asahi Kasei Hipore 25 were selected in this study due to their wide usage and different manufacturing methods [4,28]. The separator Celgard 2500 is made from PP and produced by a dry-processed method involving the uniaxial stretching along the machine direction (MD) with a nominal thickness of 25 μm, while the separator Asahi Kasei Hipore 25 is made from PE and manufactured by a wet-processed method including biaxial stretching with a nominal thickness of 25 μm. To simplify the discussion, the abbreviations of PP and PE are adopted in the rest of this paper. Their actual thicknesses are 25.06 ± 0.05 μm and 23.52 ± 0.03 μm, respectively, which were measured via a digital micrometre using the averaged value from 40 layers of separators stacked together. The measurement procedure is followed according to the TAPPI T411 standard.

To avoid errors induced by the manufacturing process in the laboratory and achieve consistent results, commercial electrodes are chosen in this study. The cathode electrode sheet used in this study is made from the aluminium foil coated with Lithium Manganese Oxide (LiMn2O4) on a single side which was purchased from HF-Kejing MTI Corporation. The single-side active material areal density is 166 g/m2 and the specific capacity is 110 mAh/g according to the manufacturer. The thicknesses of coating and aluminium foil are 85 μm and 15 μm, respectively. The commercially available lithium metal foil is selected here as the anode material in the assembly of a half-cell with more details given in Section 2.6. The half-cell is an electrochemical cell which is fabricated in the form of coin cell, but consists of active cathode material (LiMn2O4) as the working electrode and lithium metal foil as the counter electrode.

The electrolyte of 1 M LiPF6 in 3:7 volume-ratio mixture of EC (ethylene carbonate)/EMC (ethyl methyl carbonate) was purchased from dodochem.net and stored in the glove box under an Argon atmosphere before the fabrication of the half-cell.

The 3D image data of the PP and PE separator samples were captured using a Zeiss Xraida Ultra 810 housed in the Henry Moseley X-ray Imaging Facility (HMXIF) at The University of Manchester. A quasi-monochromatic beam (5.4 keV) was used to collect 509 X-ray transmission radiographs (exposure time 30s/frame) at the voxel size of 128.717 nm over the sample rotation of 180°. Subsequently, the raw CT image dataset was processed using Avizo software package (Version 2019, FEI). An automated thresholding method (the ISODATA method [29]) in Avizo software package was applied to select the high greyscale threshold for the polymer phase. The cubic 3D volumes of interest with 23.684 × 23.684 × 23.684 μm (185 × 185 × 185 voxels) and 23.040 × 23.040 × 23.040 μm (180 × 180 × 180 voxels) for PP and PE samples were selected in the present study, respectively.

The surface microstructures of uncompressed and compressed (under 90 MPa) PP and PE separators were captured using the SEM (TESCAN MIRA3 FEG SEM) with an acceleration voltage of 5 kV. A 10 nm thick layer of platinum was sputtered on the samples before conducting the tests.

The mechanical testing was conducted on a universal testing machine with a 30 kN load cell (Instron 5967). The engineering strain is calculated from the relative displacement between the two ends of the sample, which can be obtained from the crosshead displacement after the correction of machine compliance. To increase the accuracy of the strain measurement, a stack of separators with 40 layers with a diameter of 20 mm was set between the two platens. Due to the anisotropic properties of the dry-processed PP separator, the 40 layers of the separator need to be folded and stacked appropriately before they are cut by punching to ensure that all the layers are oriented in the same direction. A pre-compression of 0.5 MPa was applied to remove the gaps between all the layers. Tests were repeated three times for each type of separators and conducted at the strain-rate of 10−3 s−1. To obtain the partially compressed PP and PE separators, the compressive loads of 90 MPa, 60 MPa, 40 MPa, 30 MPa, 20 MPa and 10 MPa were applied, respectively, which were then unloaded with the same compressive loading strain-rate. Then, their instantaneous strains and the strains after the deformation has been stabilised (24 h is used here based on observations) were manually measured via a digital micrometre to ensure there is no further strain recovery for both PP and PE separators.

The porosity is defined as the fraction of pore volume to the total volume that comprises pore space and the polymer phase of the separator. There are various methods to determine the porosity of the separator, including the gravimetric method [19,30], the density method [31], the mercury intrusion method [4], the gas pycnometry [32,33], Focused Ion Beam (FIB-SEM) [6,11,34] and X-ray computed tomography [35,36]. The density method [31], which has accuracy of 10−5 g, was adopted here to determine the initial porosities of PP and PE separators due to its high accuracy and availability in the laboratory. The obtained porosities were summarized in Table 1 .

Tortuosity is a terminology to describe the sinuosity of the pore space since it can influence the transport processes in a porous medium. Several review papers related to tortuosity can be found in Refs. [37–39]. The tortuosity of a porous separator depends on its ionic path length and thickness, which provides information about the effect of pore blockage on the transport of ions. The tortuosity of the separator ( τ ) is determined by the value obtained from the measurements of effective ionic conductivity of the separator soaked in the electrolyte (
σ
e f f
), the conductivity of the bulk electrolyte (
σ 0
= 0.830
S / m
from Ref. [40]) and the porosity of the separator (
Φ
) using the following equation [41]
(1)
τ =
σ 0
σ
e f f
Φ

The effective ionic conductivity of a compressed separator saturated with the electrolyte (
σ
e f f
) (in S/m), is determined by the laws of resistance
(2)
σ
e f f
=
L 1
R
i o n
A
where
R
i o n
(in Ω) is the ionic resistance of the compressed separator saturated with electrolytes;
A
(in m2) is the electrode's area, which is the spacer's area in this study;
L 1
(in m) is the thickness of the compressed separator.

To estimate the value of
R
i o n
, a CR2032 coin cell was assembled by sandwiching the compressed separator between two stainless steel spacers and filling it with the liquid electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC/EMC with a 3:7 vol ratio). The configured coin cell is illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). Subsequently, the resistance of the assembled coin cell (R
cell ) was measured by EIS with an electrochemical workstation (CS-310, Corrtest), where a 5-mV voltage amplitude in the frequency range of 1 kHZ to 1 MHz were adopted. Based on Ohm's law, the overall resistance of the assembled coin cell,
R
c e l l
, can be expressed as
(3)
R
c e l l
=
R
s e r i e s
+
R
i o n
where
R
s e r i e s
is the series resistance of the circuit aroused by the configured coin cell with zero thickness separator, i.e.,
R
s e r i e s
only including component resistance (spring, two spaces, positive and negative cases) and interfacial resistance between electrolyte and spacers, which is determined as 0.45 Ω by gradually decreasing the separator layer number and extending the measured data (
R
c e l l
) to zero separator layer. The details of the method can be found in Ref. [42]. The whole fabrication process of the coin cell was achieved in the argon-filled environment inside a Pure Lab HE Glovebox with O2
≤
0.1 and H2O level = 0.0 ppm. To ensure the consistency of results, three cells were assembled and tested for each type of compressed separators in this study.

To investigate the effect of compressed separator on the electrochemical performance of LIB, a half-cell configuration using the CR2032 coin cell components was adopted, which consists of commercial LiMn2O4 (as the cathode), lithium metal (as the anode), electrolyte, and compressed/uncompressed separators immersed in the electrolyte. Due to the anisotropic properties of the dry-processed PP separator, the compressed separator needs to be punched into a round shape with a diameter of 18 mm before being assembled in the coin cell. The configuration of the half-cell with a compressed separator is shown in Fig. 1 (b). It is worthwhile to mention that an additional intact (i.e. uncompressed) separator was used in the half-cell in Fig. 1 (b) to avoid short circuits and achieve stable testing results. The assembled half-cells with a compressed separator are cyclically charged and discharged using BTS 4000 Neware battery tester with the galvanostatic mode and a voltage window from 3.0 to 4.5 V. The C-rate performance of the half-cell was carried out at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C and 4C where the C-rate is used to measure the speed at which a battery is fully charged or discharged (charging at a C-rate of N C means that the battery is charged from 0 to 100% in 1/N hour). Five charging-discharging cycles were performed for each C-rate and the testing results were processed using the software Neware BTSDA 7.6.

To better assess the voltage loss due to the existence of the compressed separator saturated in the electrolyte, a simplified 2D axisymmetric model was built to reduce the computation cost. The governing equation in the electrochemical modelling is based on the porous electrode theory with mass balance and charge balance in both solid and liquid phases. The governing equations describing the electrochemical reactions, charge transfer and mass transfer have been reported in Ref. [43] which will not be repeated here. The built model was numerically solved via the finite element method (FEM) in the commercial software COMSOL Multiphysics 5.4. One of the advantages of COMSOL Multiphysics is that the material properties of various electrodes and electrolytes are already embedded in the Interface of the Li-ion Battery. Therefore, those properties can be directly and conveniently adopted in our model. The extra variables as inputs into the model are thickness, porosity and tortuosity of the compressed separator, which will be given in Section 3.5.

The typical representative stress-strain curves of PP and PE separators under interrupted compression loading and subsequent unloading at a strain-rate of 10−3 s−1 are respectively presented in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). The stress is calculated using the nominal area of the separator with a diameter of 20 mm. Engineering strain is calculated from the crosshead displacement corrected by machine compliance, which is then normalized by the stacked thickness of 40 layers. The stress-strain curves in Fig. 2 (a) and (b) have different characteristics, which are associated with their manufacturing process and microstructure.

Dry-processed PP separator of Celgard 2500 has the common stress-strain curve of cellular material, which can be simplified into three regimes, i.e. the linear elastic regime, the plateau regime and the densification regime [44]. From the reconstructed 3D microstructure and the surface views of the PP separator obtained via XCT and SEM image technique shown in Fig. 2 (d) and (e), the microstructures consist of three phases, i.e., lamellae, fibrils, and voids. Our previous study has demonstrated that lamellae in PP separator resist the majority of the compressive force during the linear elastic regime and the plateau regime [36]. Furthermore, bending and collapse of lamellae in PP separator occur after a macroscopic stress-strain curve yield point (Note: the 0.2% offset rule is employed to determine the yield point (0.059, 13.19) of the PP separator and the non-linear part at the very beginning of its stress-strain curve is not included for the determination of the elastic modulus). Subsequently, the crushed lamellae start to move into the voids of the porous separator in the plateau regime. Eventually, the fibrils begin to participate in the resistance of the compression force and the porous separator starts to densify at the onset strain of densification, which can be determined by the energy absorption efficiency curve presented in Ref. [45], as quantitatively determined in the Section 3.2. The pore volume is decreased due to the widened lamellae of PP separator during the densification regime, which can be observed from the surface view of the PP separator after being compressed at 90 MPa in Fig. 2 (f) and from our previous simulation research in Ref. [36]. The samples of PP separators were changed into an oval shape from the original round shape during the compression process. The main reason for this phenomenon is due to its intrinsic anisotropy in microstructure introduced by the uniaxial stretching manufacturing process along MD.

Because the separator itself is soft and thin, it is difficult to capture the cross-sectional image via SEM, and therefore, only the surface views of the PP and PE separators were scanned. From a microstructure point of view, due to the lack of lamellae in the through-thickness direction (TTD) for the wet-processed PE separator of Hipore 25, which can be observed from 3D microstructure of PE separator in Fig. 2 (g), the linear elastic regime in Fig. 2 (b) is not obvious. The circular shape of PE separator sample is maintained, indicating isotropic deformation due to its biaxial stretching manufacturing method. The surface views of the PE separator before and after 90 MPa compressive loading were obtained via SEM, as shown in Fig. 2 (h) and (i), respectively. The widened lamellae of the PE separator and decreased pore area on the surface were observed as well.

To quantify the deformation of compressed separators, the compressive strains of separator samples after unloading from the specified compressive loading stresses are summarized in Fig. 2 (c) and listed in Table 2 . It should be noted that the strains of PP separators after unloading were directly read from Fig. 2 (a), while the strains of PE separators after unloading were measured at 24 h after the test since the material has obvious viscoelasticity. From Fig. 2 (c), the PP separator has better resistance of compressive loading than the PE separator under compressive stress below 40 MPa, while the PE separator shows a slightly greater resistance of compression than the PP separator for stress above 40 MPa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strain at the onset of densification of separators
ce:section.ce:para:
The compressive behaviour of the porous separators can be divided into three regimes, i.e., elastic regime, plateau regime and densification regime. The plateau regime ends at the onset strain of densification,
ε
c d
, which also represents the start of densification.

Based on the energy absorption efficiency methods (see Ref. [45] for details and the applications), the onset strain of densification (
ε
c d
), at which the plateau regime terminates and the energy absorption efficiency reaches a maximum on the efficiency-strain curve, is determined by
(5)
d η
( ε )
d ε
|
ε =
ε
c d
= 0
where
η
( ε )
is the energy absorption efficiency and determined by
(6)
η
( ε )
=
1
σ

∫ 0 ε
σ
( ε )
d ε

The stress-strain curves of PP and PE separator under 90 MPa compression loading conditions are shown in Fig. 3 (a). By using Eqs. (5) and (6), the energy absorption efficiency curves for PP and PE separators are illustrated in Fig. 3 (b). The obtained the onset strain of densification of PP and PE separators are 0.374 and 0.358, respectively, which are used in Fig. 2 (c) and the following discussion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of porosity and tortuosity under uniaxial compression
ce:section.ce:para:
It has been shown that the matrix materials of the PP and PE separators have relatively high Poisson's ratio of 0.42 [46], which is close to 0.5 for incompressible materials. Therefore, the solid volume of the polymer separator can be considered as a constant during the compressive test based on the mass conservation [47]. Meanwhile, although the separator experiences large deformation, the deformation in its matrix is either small or concentrated in a small matrix volume because it mainly involves bending deformation, which further justifies the assumption that the solid volume of the polymer separator is a constant during deformation. Hence, the relationship between porosity and the corresponding compressive strain of the compressed separator can be derived by equating the solid volume of polymer material in the compressed and uncompressed separators, i.e.
(7)
V 1
=
A 1
L 1
(
1 −
Φ 1
)
=
V 0
=
A 0
L 0
(
1 −
Φ 0
)
where
V 1
,
A 1
,
L 1
and
Φ 1
are respectively the current volume, surface area, thickness and porosity of the separator after compression;
V 0
,
A 0
,
L 0
and
Φ 0
are respectively the initial volume, surface area, thickness and porosity of the separator before compression. The surface area of the separator can be considered as a constant for the compressed and uncompressed separators because the compressed oval/circular area of separator is almost equal to the original circular shape of the separator. Thus, the porosity of the compressed separator can be expressed as a function of engineering strain
(8)
Φ 1
= 1 −
L 0
L 1
(
1 −
Φ 0
)
=
Φ 0
− ε
1 − ε

Substituting the initial porosity of
Φ 0
(0.52) obtained from the density method in Section 2.4 and the engineering strain (
ε
) obtained from compressive test into Eq. (8), analytical predictions for porosity variation of PP and PE separators against the compressive strain were obtained in Fig. 4 (a).

The relationship between porosity and tortuosity plays an important role in the electrochemical performance of the separator under compressive loading conditions. Therefore, a generalized form of Bruggeman relation modified by Thorat et al. [50] is used here to correlate the tortuosity and porosity
(9)
τ 2
= γ
Φ
(
1 − α
)
where
τ 2
is also termed as ‘tortuosity factor’ in some references (e.g. Ref. [51]);
γ
is the scaling factor;
α
is the Bruggeman exponent which depends on the morphology of the porous separator. Fig. 4 (e) shows the fitted curves for both separators using Eq. (9). The values of scaling factor (
γ
) and Bruggeman exponent ( α ) are (0.106, 4.741) and (0.289, 2.829) for PP and PE separators, respectively. Lastly, by combining the Eqs. (8) and (9), tortuosity can be directly determined from the initial porosity and the engineering compressive strain (
ε
), i.e.
(10)
τ 2
= γ
(
Φ 0
− ε
1 − ε
)
(
1 − α
)
when both
γ
and
α
are specified as material constants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of half-cell assembled with compressed separator
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a) to (f) respectively show the charge and discharge curves under various C-rates for half-cells assembled with uncompressed and compressed Celgard 2500 PP separators at different compressive stresses. All the half-cells were charged and discharged with a C-rate between 0.1C and 4C. Fig. 5 (a) shows the charge and discharge curves of the half-cell with the two uncompressed PP separator which is regarded as the benchmark cell. Fig. 5 (b) illustrates that the charge and discharge curves of half-cell with PP separators compressed by 20 MPa stress are similar to those of the benchmark cell, showing that the battery's capacity (density), i.e. the capacity normalized by the mass of the active material of the cathode), only has a slight decrease at 4C. As shown in Fig. 5 (c) and (d), the capacities of half-cell with separators compressed by 30 MPa and 40 MPa stresses are nearly unchanged at 0.1C and 0.2C compared with those of the benchmark cell; however, the capacities of half-cell decreases obviously at C-rates of 1C, 2C and 4C. In Fig. 5 (e), the capacities of the half-cell with separator compressed by 60 MPa stress decrease dramatically at 0.2C, 0.5C and 1C, while the half-cell still maintains a capacity of 95 mAh/g at 0.1C. Furthermore, the capacities at 2C and 4C cannot be measured in that cell, which is mainly due to the higher resistance of the compressed separator. As shown in Fig. 5 (f), the capacity of half-cell with separator compressed by 90 MPa stress can only be measured at 0.1C and its capacity is 5 mAh/g, which is very low. From the microstructure view, this result can be attributed to the higher tortuosity and lower porosity of the largely compressed PP separator at 90 MPa.

It should be noted that our focused problem is the influence of the ‘state’ of the separator's pore and connection on the performance of the battery. Strictly speaking, this is different from the influence of the ‘instantaneous pore/connection during operation’ on the battery's performance. However, as the ion transport speed is much higher than the deformation speed (or rate) in a charge/discharge cycle, we believe it is legitimate to apply our ‘state-based’ results in the battery operation although there are some other issues (e.g. the electrolyte intake in operation may be different from that in our experiment), which need to be determined by other means. Therefore, the evolution of the active stress is not considered in the present study.

Fig. 6 (a) to (f) show the charge and discharge of half-cell with compressed PE separators at specified compressive stresses. In Fig. 6 (a), the charge and discharge curves of the half-cell with uncompressed PE separator are regarded as benchmark curves. As shown in Fig. 6 (b)–(e) and compared with the charge and discharge curves of half-cell with compressed PP separators in Fig. 5 (b)–(e), similar trends are observed in half-cell with compressed PE separators. However, it is interesting to observe that the capacities of half-cells with compressed PE separators at 60 MPa and 90 MPa can still be measured if their C-rates are not higher than 2C and 0.5C, respectively. For example, half-cells of compressed PE separators for 60 MPa and 90 MPa compressions respectively have 120 mAh/g and 70 mAh/g capacities at 0.1C in Fig. 6 (e) and (f). Furthermore, in Fig. 6 (e) and (f), the capacities of half-cells with compressed PE separators at 60 MPa and 90 MPa start to significantly decline at 0.5C and 0.2C, respectively. Therefore, it can be concluded that the compressed separators will significantly affect the capacity performance of LIB, especially for larger compressions and higher C-rates.

In order to quantitatively determine how the compressed separators affect the capacity performance of a half-cell, the capacity-strain curves at different C-rate were plotted in Fig. 7 based on Figs. 2 (c), Figs. 5 and 6. In Fig. 7 (a), the capacity of half-cell with compressed PP separator before entering the densification regime is stable under 0.1C, 0.2C and 0.5C. It is noted that the capacity of half-cell with compressed PP separator is sensitive to the compressive strain in the plateau regime under 1C and 2C. In Fig. 7 (b), the capacity of half-cell with compressed PE separator is almost constant in the plateau regime. In addition, although the PE separator starts to densify at the onset strain of densification, the half-cells still maintain their capacity density to a certain degree. This is due to the fact that the compressed PE separator still maintains the passages to allow the transport of ions, which is also the reason why the electric tortuosity [42] can still be measured in our EIS testing. Therefore, it can be inferred that the tortuosity of the separator plays a significant role in the performance of LIB, especially for a LIB having a separator with high tortuosity and working under a high C-rate. Furthermore, by introducing a threshold capacity as a percentage of the maximum capacity of a cell, the maximum C-rate can be determined for a battery. For example, if the threshold percentage is 72% (i.e. 85 mAh/g)) for a battery, a battery with compressed separator should be charged/discharged below 1C and the critical strains of a compressed PP and PE separators are 0.285 and 0.365, respectively. The former is slightly smaller than
ε
c d
= 0.374 for PP separator, while the latter is very close to
ε
c d
= 0.358 for PE separator.

To assess how the compressed PP and PE separators affect the electrochemical performance of LIB and validate the experimental observation in Section 3.4, a 2D asymmetrical half-cell model shown in Fig. 8 (a) was built and solved numerically in COMSOL Multiphysics software. The simulation parameters for cathode material (LiMn2O4) and electrolyte (1.2 M LiPF6 in a 3:7 mass-ratio mixture of EC and EMC) used in the electrochemical performance modelling in Section 2.6 are obtained from Material Library embedded in the software and listed in Table 3 , while the concentration dependent parameters used for the electrolyte in our model, including electrolyte diffusion coefficient, electrolyte conductivity, electrolyte activity and cathodic transport number, are not listed in the Table 3 for brevity, which can be found in Ref. [52]. The model was discharged from 0.1C to 4C (the applied current of 1C is 1.93 mA). To determine the effect of a compressed separator on the electrochemical performance of the battery cell, the thickness, porosity and tortuosity of the separator (listed in Table 4 ) were varied as the input parameters while all other parameters were kept identical. The values of voltage loss at the beginning of discharge in the electrolyte across the compressed separator, which is the difference of electrolyte potential between the top and bottom interfaces of the separator [53], were selected as the outputs for comparison. The voltage loss is caused by the Li-ion gradient in the electrolyte, which influences the Li-ion mobility and thereby the conductivity of the electrolyte.

The voltage losses across the PP and PE separators in the model at different C-rates for different compression strains were normalized by the designed voltage window range of charge and discharge used in the experiments in Section 2.5 (i.e.
Δ V = 1.5
V
), and respectively plotted at different C-rates in Fig. 8 (b) and (c). It shows that the voltage losses in the electrolyte across PP and PE separators increase with the increase of the compressive strain and C-rate. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (d), the increase of the compressive strain increases the tortuosity and decreases the porosity, and thus, the resistance of the compressed separator (immersed in electrolyte) increases. As a result, the voltage loss across the separator of LIB increases with the compressive strain (i.e. the voltage loss is positively correlated to the tortuosity and negatively correlated to the porosity and thickness.). On the other hand, for a compressed separator immersed in the electrolyte, its resistance is fixed across different C-rates. Therefore, the voltage loss is increased when the C-rate is increased according to Ohm's law. The onset strain of densification represents a turning point for the start of rapid voltage loss rate, especially when the C-rate is beyond 0.2C, which can be attributed to the large resistance induced by the compressed PP separator. This phenomenon is consistent with the experimental observation in Fig. 5 (e), in which the capacity of half-cell cannot be measured at higher C-rate (i.e. 2C and 4C).

In addition to the general observations and mechanisms described in the above paragraph, the relationships between the voltage loss and the compressive strain for PP separator in Fig. 8 (b) and PE separator in Fig. 8 (c) have different features. The onset strain of densification for the PP separator (
ε
c d
= 0.374) represents a turning point for the start of rapid voltage loss rate, while at
ε
c d
= 0.358 the voltage loss in a compressed PE separator has a slowly increasing trend and the turning point occurs at the compression strain of 0.41. This difference between compressed PP and PE separators can be explained by their different compressive deformation mechanism due to their unique microstructures. The PP separator has an anisotropic property and a large number of MD-oriented fibrils due to the uniaxial stretching manufacturing process, which means that the PP separator can be easily densified after the onset strain of densification, and thus, leads to a higher tortuosity [36]. However, PE separators have an isotropic property and random-oriented fine fibrils due to the biaxial stretching manufacturing process, leading to a delayed and relatively mild increase in the tortuosity of PE separators in the densification regime. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4 (d) for the fitted curve of tortuosity against the strain, the compressed PP separator has a much higher tortuosity than the compressed PE separator at same large compressive strain. Hence, it is concluded that the pore connectivity is very crucial for a compressed separator to maintain its ion transport function as a key component of a LIB.

Lastly, to allow for the results shown Fig. 8 (b) and (c) to be extended to other separators whose materials and morphologies may be different from the those used here, the normalized voltage loss (VL) at the beginning of discharge can be expressed by a non-dimensional number (
τ 2
L ‾
1
Φ
) based on the Ohm's law and Eqs. (1) and (2), i.e.
(11)
V L
=
I R
Δ V
=
I
τ 2
L 1
Δ V
Φ σ
0
A
=
τ 2
L ‾
1
Φ
where
L ‾
1
=
L 1
I
σ 0
Δ V A
;
L 1
is the thickness of the compressed separator;
I
is the applied current;
σ 0
is the conductivity of the electrolyte used in the model (given in Table 3);
A
is the area of the separator in the model (
I / A
is the current density);
Δ V
is the voltage window range of charge and discharge which is 1.5 V here. Then, the linear relationship between
V L
and
τ 2
L ‾
1
Φ
, together with the calculated data from Fig. 8 (b) and (c), is shown in Fig. 8 (d). It can be seen that the numerical results are fairly close to the predictions from Eq. (11), especially when
τ 2
L ‾
1
Φ
is small. Therefore, the voltage loss can be reasonable predicted via the non-dimensional number (
τ 2
L ‾
1
Φ
) and can be generalized to other deformed battery separators.

The effects of compressed PP and PE separators on the performance of LIB were experimentally investigated and validated by a numerical model. The main findings are.
(i)
With the increased compressive deformations of PP and PE separators, the transports of Li-ions across the separators are impeded. Based on the capacity performance of LIB with compressed PP/PE measured at different C-rates, the onset strain of densification of a porous separator is found to be a critical deformation parameter (0.374 for PP and 0.358 for PE) to influence the performance of LIB, which may be useful when dealing with the recycling and detection of a defective LIB in near future. The C-rate of charging/discharging a LIB with compressed separator should be applied below 1C to maintain its expected performance.
(ii)
A semi-analytical relation between separators' tortuosity and compressive strain is proposed and coefficients for PP and PE separators are experimentally determined. Tortuosity is a key input parameter for the use of homogenous battery model. The tortuosity of compressed PP separator is higher than that of PE separator under given compressive stress due to their distinct microstructure.
(iii)
The computational modelling results obtained from COMSOL Multiphysics software can reasonably explain the electrochemical test results in terms of the voltage loss in the electrolyte across the compressed separators. This modelling method provides a useful and efficient tool to allow researchers to understand the roles of various influential factors (including the compressive strain of the separator) on the electrochemical performance of LIB through parametric analyses. A non-dimensional number is proposed to evaluate the influence of separator's geometrical parameters (thickness, porosity and tortuosity) on battery's voltage loss.

The research method proposed for the study of the electrochemical performance of LIB with compressed PP and PE separators may be extended to other batteries and more general loading conditions that a battery separator may experience. Therefore, the influence of the separator deformation under complex stress states on the battery performance can be evaluated.

Wei Sun: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Q.M. Li: Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Ping Xiao: Supervision, Resources. Paola Carbone: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The XCT and SEM images were supported by the Henry Royce Institute for Advanced Materials, funded through EPSRC grants EP/R00661X/1, EP/S019367/1, EP/P025021/1 and EP/P025498/1. The authors acknowledge the Henry Mosley X-ray Imaging Facility, especially Dr Julia Behnsen for the technical support, advice and assistance provided. In addition, the authors also would like to acknowledge Mr Francis Moissinac's help with the electrochemical experimental setup and suggestions, and Dr Wenji Yang's help with SEM characterization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tortuosity in porous media: a critical review
